DATA IMPORTATION, CLEANING AND WRANGLING/MANIPULATION¶

1. DATA IMPORTATION¶

1.1. Install and Laod the Required Libararies¶

In [1]:
#!pip install pandas
#!pip install numpy
#!pip install matplotlib
#!pip install seaborn

1.2. Loading the Libraries¶

In [2]:
import pandas as pd                         ## For data impportation and handling data frames
import numpy as np                          ## For handling numeric operations
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt             ## For data visualization
import seaborn as sn                        ## For data visualization

### Filter warnings
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

1.3. Reading the Dataset into Jupyter Notebook¶

In [3]:
df = pd.read_csv("GSSsubset1.csv")

1.4. General Data Inspection¶

1.4.1. Check the Column Names¶
In [4]:
print(f"Columns: {df.columns.tolist()}")

Columns: ['id', 'sex', 'degree', 'income', 'marital', 'age', 'height', 'weight', 'hrswrk']
1.4.2. Check the Shape of the Data¶
In [5]:
print(f"Shape: {df.shape}")

Shape: (3000, 9)
1.4.3. Check the Variable Typpes¶
In [6]:
df.info()

<class 'pandas.DataFrame'>
RangeIndex: 3000 entries, 0 to 2999
Data columns (total 9 columns):
 #   Column   Non-Null Count  Dtype  
---  ------   --------------  -----  
 0   id       3000 non-null   int64  
 1   sex      3000 non-null   str    
 2   degree   2942 non-null   str    
 3   income   2767 non-null   float64
 4   marital  2934 non-null   str    
 5   age      3000 non-null   int64  
 6   height   3000 non-null   float64
 7   weight   2879 non-null   float64
 8   hrswrk   2933 non-null   float64
dtypes: float64(4), int64(2), str(3)
memory usage: 211.1 KB
1.4.4. Check the First Few Observations¶
In [7]:
df.head(20)
Out[7]:
id sex degree income marital age height weight hrswrk
0 1 MALE HIGH SCHOOL 26722.56 NEVER MARRIED 18 73.2 86.3 35.0
1 2 MALE NaN 36544.91 MARRIED 35 69.9 95.3 47.0
2 3 FEMALE HIGH SCHOOL 59587.14 DIVORCED 28 70.1 60.9 16.0

19 20 FEMALE BACHELOR 84696.68 DIVORCED 63 59.7 65.5 42.0

df.describe()

count 3000.000000 2767.000000 3000.000000 3000.000000 2879.000000 2933.000000
mean 1500.500000 63871.815790 42.078000 67.400500 76.389232 39.778043

### Remove the id Column from the Summary Statistics
df.drop(columns = ["id"]).describe()

income age height weight hrswrk

sex
FEMALE    1563
MALE      1437

marital

2. DATA CLEANING¶

2.1. Handling Missing Values¶

In [14]:
df.head(20)
Out[14]:
id sex degree income marital age height weight hrswrk
0 1 MALE HIGH SCHOOL 26722.56 NEVER MARRIED 18 73.2 86.3 35.0
1 2 MALE NaN 36544.91 MARRIED 35 69.9 95.3 47.0
2 3 FEMALE HIGH SCHOOL 59587.14 DIVORCED 28 70.1 60.9 16.0
3 4 FEMALE GRADUATE NaN MARRIED 43 61.9 76.0 44.0
4 5 MALE HIGH SCHOOL 48899.47 MARRIED 48 65.8 58.9 44.0
5 6 FEMALE HIGH SCHOOL 47790.38 MARRIED 43 60.7 68.4 46.0
6 7 FEMALE BACHELOR 102903.23 MARRIED 42 66.5 66.7 51.0
7 8 MALE HIGH SCHOOL 36707.09 NEVER MARRIED 52 65.9 76.8 48.0
8 9 FEMALE BACHELOR 48404.51 MARRIED 35 66.8 94.6 28.0
9 10 MALE HIGH SCHOOL 29044.70 NEVER MARRIED 18 69.3 85.2 NaN
10 11 FEMALE GRADUATE 419380.86 SEPARATED 58 62.4 64.8 31.0
11 12 MALE JUNIOR COLLEGE 14995.63 WIDOWED 40 66.8 68.7 45.0
12 13 FEMALE LT HIGH SCHOOL 22832.15 WIDOWED 52 61.3 61.0 25.0
13 14 MALE LT HIGH SCHOOL 23183.92 NaN 50 71.6 100.4 55.0
14 15 FEMALE GRADUATE 114886.28 MARRIED 40 64.1 75.5 42.0

16 17 MALE BACHELOR NaN NEVER MARRIED 24 69.3 84.9 50.0
17 18 FEMALE HIGH SCHOOL 37509.86 SEPARATED 49 61.5 58.8 31.0

19 20 FEMALE BACHELOR 84696.68 DIVORCED 63 59.7 65.5 42.0
2.1.1. Check the Presence of Missing Values¶
In [15]:
print("="*110)
print("Checking the Presence of Missing Values in the General Social Survey Data")
print("="*110)

print(df.isna().sum())

==============================================================================================================
Checking the Presence of Missing Values in the General Social Survey Data
==============================================================================================================
id           0
sex          0
degree      58
income     233
marital     66
age          0
height       0
weight     121
hrswrk      67
dtype: int64
In [16]:
print("="*110)
print("Checking the Percentage of Missing Values per Variable in the General Social Survey Data")
print("="*110)

Missing_values = df.isna().mean().round(4)*100
Missing_values

==============================================================================================================
Checking the Percentage of Missing Values per Variable in the General Social Survey Data
==============================================================================================================
Out[16]:
id         0.00
sex        0.00
degree     1.93
income     7.77
marital    2.20
age        0.00
height     0.00
weight     4.03
hrswrk     2.23
dtype: float64
2.1.2. Methods of Handling Missing Values¶
  • Complete cases Analysis- Commonly used approach but Risky
  • Mean and/or Median Imputation - For symmetric and asymmetric data
  • Mode Imputation - For Categorical and Character data
2.1.3. Complete Cases Analysis (Row Deletion)¶
In [17]:
df.info()

<class 'pandas.DataFrame'>
RangeIndex: 3000 entries, 0 to 2999
Data columns (total 9 columns):
 #   Column   Non-Null Count  Dtype  
---  ------   --------------  -----  
 0   id       3000 non-null   int64  
 1   sex      3000 non-null   str    
 2   degree   2942 non-null   str    
 3   income   2767 non-null   float64
 4   marital  2934 non-null   str    
 5   age      3000 non-null   int64  
 6   height   3000 non-null   float64
 7   weight   2879 non-null   float64
 8   hrswrk   2933 non-null   float64
dtypes: float64(4), int64(2), str(3)
memory usage: 211.1 KB
In [18]:
df_drop_all = df.dropna()
print(df_drop_all.isna().sum())

id         0
sex        0
degree     0
income     0
marital    0
age        0
height     0
weight     0
hrswrk     0
dtype: int64

df_drop_all.info()

<class 'pandas.DataFrame'>

2.1.4. Mean - Imputation (for symmetric data)¶
In [20]:
## Identify the Numeric Columns
df_mean = df.copy()
df_mean.head()
Out[20]:
id sex degree income marital age height weight hrswrk
0 1 MALE HIGH SCHOOL 26722.56 NEVER MARRIED 18 73.2 86.3 35.0
1 2 MALE NaN 36544.91 MARRIED 35 69.9 95.3 47.0
2 3 FEMALE HIGH SCHOOL 59587.14 DIVORCED 28 70.1 60.9 16.0
3 4 FEMALE GRADUATE NaN MARRIED 43 61.9 76.0 44.0
4 5 MALE HIGH SCHOOL 48899.47 MARRIED 48 65.8 58.9 44.0
In [21]:
df_mean.info()

<class 'pandas.DataFrame'>
RangeIndex: 3000 entries, 0 to 2999
Data columns (total 9 columns):
 #   Column   Non-Null Count  Dtype  
---  ------   --------------  -----  
 0   id       3000 non-null   int64  
 1   sex      3000 non-null   str    
 2   degree   2942 non-null   str    
 3   income   2767 non-null   float64
 4   marital  2934 non-null   str    
 5   age      3000 non-null   int64  
 6   height   3000 non-null   float64
 7   weight   2879 non-null   float64
 8   hrswrk   2933 non-null   float64
dtypes: float64(4), int64(2), str(3)
memory usage: 211.1 KB
In [22]:
numeric_cols = df_mean.select_dtypes(include = "number").columns
df_mean[numeric_cols] = df_mean[numeric_cols].fillna(df_mean[numeric_cols].mean())
In [23]:
print(df.isna().sum())

id           0
sex          0
degree      58
income     233
marital     66
age          0
height       0
weight     121
hrswrk      67
dtype: int64
In [24]:
print(df_mean.isna().sum())

id          0
sex         0
degree     58
income      0
marital    66
age         0
height      0
weight      0
hrswrk      0
dtype: int64
2.1.5. Median Imputation for Asymmetric Data¶
In [25]:
df_median = df.copy()
df_median.head()
Out[25]:
id sex degree income marital age height weight hrswrk
0 1 MALE HIGH SCHOOL 26722.56 NEVER MARRIED 18 73.2 86.3 35.0
1 2 MALE NaN 36544.91 MARRIED 35 69.9 95.3 47.0
2 3 FEMALE HIGH SCHOOL 59587.14 DIVORCED 28 70.1 60.9 16.0
3 4 FEMALE GRADUATE NaN MARRIED 43 61.9 76.0 44.0
4 5 MALE HIGH SCHOOL 48899.47 MARRIED 48 65.8 58.9 44.0
In [26]:
numeric_cols = df_median.select_dtypes(include = "number").columns
df_median[numeric_cols] = df_median[numeric_cols].fillna(df_median[numeric_cols].median())
In [27]:
print(df_median.isna().sum())

id          0
sex         0
degree     58
income      0
marital    66
age         0
height      0
weight      0
hrswrk      0
dtype: int64
2.1.5. Mode Imputation for Categorical and Character Data¶
In [28]:
df["degree"].value_counts()
Out[28]:
degree
HIGH SCHOOL       1094
LT HIGH SCHOOL     583
BACHELOR           566
JUNIOR COLLEGE     465
GRADUATE           234
Name: count, dtype: int64
In [29]:
df["sex"].value_counts()
Out[29]:
sex
FEMALE    1563
MALE      1437
Name: count, dtype: int64
In [30]:
df["marital"].value_counts()
Out[30]:
marital
MARRIED          1518
NEVER MARRIED     691
DIVORCED          382
WIDOWED           227
SEPARATED         116
Name: count, dtype: int64
In [31]:
cat_cols = df.select_dtypes(include = ["object", "category"]).columns
for col in cat_cols:
    mode_val = df[col].mode()
    if not mode_val.empty:
        df[col]= df[col].fillna(mode_val[0])
In [32]:
df.isna().sum()
Out[32]:
id           0
sex          0
degree       0
income     233
marital      0
age          0
height       0
weight     121
hrswrk      67
dtype: int64
In [33]:
df["degree"].value_counts()
Out[33]:
degree
HIGH SCHOOL       1152
LT HIGH SCHOOL     583
BACHELOR           566
JUNIOR COLLEGE     465
GRADUATE           234
Name: count, dtype: int64
In [34]:
numeric_cols = df.select_dtypes(include = "number").columns
df[numeric_cols] = df[numeric_cols].fillna(df[numeric_cols].mean())
2.1.7. Check the Presence of Missing Values after Imputation¶
In [35]:
df.isna().sum()
Out[35]:
id         0
sex        0
degree     0
income     0
marital    0
age        0
height     0
weight     0
hrswrk     0
dtype: int64

2.2. Handling the Duplicate Entries¶

2.2.1. Check the Duplicate Entries¶

dir(df)

2.2.1. Drop the Duplicate Entries if any Exists¶
In [39]:
clean_df = df.drop_duplicates()
clean_df.info()

2.3. Handling Outliers¶

Outliers are values that lie abnormally very low or very high (far off the trend)

In [40]:
df.head(40)
Out[40]:

2.3.1. Check the Presence of Ouliers¶
In [41]:
numeric_cols = df.select_dtypes(include = ["float64", "int64"]).columns
for col in numeric_cols:
    Q1 = df[col].quantile(0.25)
    Q3 = df[col].quantile(0.75)
    IQR = Q3 - Q1
    lower_bound = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR
    upper_bound = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR

    outliers = df[(df[col] < lower_bound) | (df[col] > upper_bound)]

    print(f"Columns '{col}': {len(outliers)} Outliers")

Columns 'id': 0 Outliers
Columns 'income': 201 Outliers
Columns 'age': 10 Outliers
Columns 'height': 2 Outliers
Columns 'weight': 32 Outliers
Columns 'hrswrk': 155 Outliers
2.3.2. Print the IDs Where the Values are Considered Ouliers¶
In [42]:
numeric_cols = ["income", "age", "height", "weight", "hrswrk"]
outlier_id = {}
for col in numeric_cols:
    Q1 = df[col].quantile(0.25)
    Q3 = df[col].quantile(0.75)
    IQR = Q3 - Q1
    lower_bound = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR
    upper_bound = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR

    outliers = df[(df[col] < lower_bound) | (df[col] > upper_bound)]

    outlier_id[col] = outliers["id"].tolist()

    print(f"Column '{col}': {len(outliers)} outliers")
    print(outliers['id'].tolist())
    print("-"*110)

2.3.3. Cap the Outliers¶
In [43]:
numeric_cols = df.select_dtypes(include = ["float64", "int64"]).columns
for col in numeric_cols:
    Q1 = df[col].quantile(0.25)
    Q3 = df[col].quantile(0.75)
    IQR = Q3 - Q1
    lower_bound = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR
    upper_bound = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR

    ## Cap the Outliers 
    df[col] = np.where(df[col] < lower_bound, lower_bound, df[col])
    df[col] = np.where(df[col] > upper_bound, upper_bound, df[col])
2.3.4. Check the Presence of Outliers After Capping¶
In [44]:
numeric_cols = df.select_dtypes(include = ["float64", "int64"]).columns
for col in numeric_cols:
    Q1 = df[col].quantile(0.25)
    Q3 = df[col].quantile(0.75)
    IQR = Q3 - Q1
    lower_bound = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR
    upper_bound = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR

    outliers = df[(df[col] < lower_bound) | (df[col] > upper_bound)]

    print(f"Columns '{col}': {len(outliers)} Outliers")

Columns 'id': 0 Outliers
Columns 'income': 0 Outliers
Columns 'age': 0 Outliers
Columns 'height': 0 Outliers
Columns 'weight': 0 Outliers
Columns 'hrswrk': 0 Outliers

2.5. Save the Data as Clean Dataset¶

In [46]:
df.to_csv("Cleaned_gss_data.csv")

3. DATA MANIPULATION¶

  • Selecting (Column Selection)
  • Fitering (Subsetting Rows)
  • groupby
  • Aggregating and Summarizing
  • Combining Filter and Selection
In [47]:
df =pd.read_csv("GSSsubset.csv")
df.head()
Out[47]:
id sex degree income marital age height weight hrswrk
0 1 MALE BACHELOR 60967.50 DIVORCED 53 72 190 60
1 2 FEMALE BACHELOR 60967.50 MARRIED 26 60 97 40
2 4 FEMALE BACHELOR 10161.25 MARRIED 56 68 160 20
3 14 FEMALE HIGH SCHOOL 17551.25 MARRIED 40 65 156 37
4 16 MALE HIGH SCHOOL 17551.25 MARRIED 56 66 210 6

3.1. FILTERING¶

Subset rows that meet certain conditions

In [48]:
### Create male participants data
male = df[df['sex'] == 'MALE']
male.head()
Out[48]:
id sex degree income marital age height weight hrswrk
0 1 MALE BACHELOR 60967.50 DIVORCED 53 72 190 60
4 16 MALE HIGH SCHOOL 17551.25 MARRIED 56 66 210 6
5 19 MALE LT HIGH SCHOOL 15703.75 MARRIED 51 68 170 50
7 27 MALE HIGH SCHOOL 17551.25 SEPARATED 35 70 180 40
8 28 MALE BACHELOR 73900.00 MARRIED 57 71 225 40

3.2. MULTIPLE FILTERING¶

In [49]:
subset = df[(df['sex'] == 'FEMALE') &
    (df['age'] <35)&
    (df['marital'] == "MARRIED")&
    (df['degree'] == "GRADUATE")]
subset.head()
Out[49]:

3.4. SELECTION¶

Columns Selection

3.4.1. Single Column Selection¶

In [52]:
income_col = df['income']
income_col.head()
Out[52]:
0    60967.50
1    60967.50
2    10161.25
3    17551.25
4    17551.25
Name: income, dtype: float64

3.4.2. Select Multiple Columns¶

In [53]:
### First Option
cols = ["sex", "age", "income", "marital"]
selected_cols = df[cols]
selected_cols.head()
Out[53]:
sex age income marital
0 MALE 53 60967.50 DIVORCED
1 FEMALE 26 60967.50 MARRIED

4 MALE 56 17551.25 MARRIED

3.5. GROUP BY AND SUMMARIZE¶

In [57]:
print("="*110)
print("GROUP BY AND SUMMARIZING")
print("="*110)

print("Mean Income by Education Levels")

income_by_educ = df.groupby('degree')['income'].mean().sort_values(ascending = False)
income_by_educ

GROUP BY AND SUMMARIZING

LT HIGH SCHOOL    18412.616883

GROUP BY AND SUMMARIZING

Mean Income by Marital
Out[59]:
marital
MARRIED          43838.108058
DIVORCED         39881.177721

WIDOWED          23827.618056
Name: income, dtype: float64

3.5.1. Multiple Grouping by and Summarizing¶

In [60]:
income_by_educ = df.groupby(['sex', 'degree'])['income'].mean()
income_by_educ
Out[60]:
sex     degree        
FEMALE  BACHELOR          34269.353010
        GRADUATE          52381.502655
        HIGH SCHOOL       21630.135693
        JUNIOR COLLEGE    22180.077273
        LT HIGH SCHOOL    11350.578125
MALE    BACHELOR          60334.881241
        GRADUATE          82374.545373
        HIGH SCHOOL       34749.177272
        JUNIOR COLLEGE    43066.722973
        LT HIGH SCHOOL    23434.511111
Name: income, dtype: float64

3.5.2. Reset the Index for the Series¶

In [61]:
income_by_educ = df.groupby(['sex', 'degree'])['income'].mean().reset_index()
income_by_educ
Out[61]:
sex degree income
0 FEMALE BACHELOR 34269.353010
1 FEMALE GRADUATE 52381.502655
2 FEMALE HIGH SCHOOL 21630.135693
3 FEMALE JUNIOR COLLEGE 22180.077273
4 FEMALE LT HIGH SCHOOL 11350.578125
5 MALE BACHELOR 60334.881241
6 MALE GRADUATE 82374.545373
7 MALE HIGH SCHOOL 34749.177272
8 MALE JUNIOR COLLEGE 43066.722973
9 MALE LT HIGH SCHOOL 23434.511111

3.5.3. Unstack the Table¶

In [62]:
income_by_educ = df.groupby(['sex','marital', 'degree'])['income'].mean().unstack()
income_by_educ
Out[62]:
degree BACHELOR GRADUATE HIGH SCHOOL JUNIOR COLLEGE LT HIGH SCHOOL
sex marital
FEMALE DIVORCED 39995.969556 44455.468750 22684.660714 25310.750000 15149.500000
MARRIED 34316.479846 54501.250000 24494.319813 19109.576087 9131.928571
NEVER MARRIED 28860.589286 52449.618143 18487.483108 23555.625000 11131.187500
SEPARATED 88680.000000 40645.000000 18490.395833 24941.250000 8313.750000
WIDOWED 30021.875000 NaN 18746.691176 NaN 15565.187500
MALE DIVORCED 56866.050000 109717.882909 39306.581451 61363.392857 30252.812500
MARRIED 70023.384629 86758.384400 44076.445458 41825.347222 23618.440000
NEVER MARRIED 39767.437500 43550.613636 21546.168831 34086.375000 19715.464286
SEPARATED NaN 68531.425500 15080.218750 20322.500000 16812.250000
WIDOWED 22862.812500 88680.000000 26683.178571 49882.500000 NaN

3.6. ADVANCED GROUPING AND MULTIPLE AGGREGATION¶

In [63]:
summary = df.groupby('degree').agg({
    'income':['mean','median','std','var','count'],
    'age':['mean', 'median'],
    'height':['mean','median']
})
summary
Out[63]:
income age height
mean median std var count mean median mean median
degree
BACHELOR 47738.621187 40645.00 38036.482608 1.446774e+09 209 44.550239 44.0 68.057416 68.0
GRADUATE 68457.773552 49882.50 47526.992948 2.258815e+09 125 47.624000 47.0 68.176000 69.0
HIGH SCHOOL 28309.892098 24017.50 26671.333894 7.113601e+08 491 43.602851 43.0 67.173116 67.0
JUNIOR COLLEGE 30580.141304 27712.50 20292.993851 4.118056e+08 92 42.413043 42.5 66.858696 67.0
LT HIGH SCHOOL 18412.616883 15703.75 13324.116851 1.775321e+08 77 47.363636 49.0 66.636364 66.0

4. FEATURES ENGINEERING¶

In [64]:
df.head()
Out[64]:
id sex degree income marital age height weight hrswrk
0 1 MALE BACHELOR 60967.50 DIVORCED 53 72 190 60
1 2 FEMALE BACHELOR 60967.50 MARRIED 26 60 97 40
2 4 FEMALE BACHELOR 10161.25 MARRIED 56 68 160 20
3 14 FEMALE HIGH SCHOOL 17551.25 MARRIED 40 65 156 37
4 16 MALE HIGH SCHOOL 17551.25 MARRIED 56 66 210 6

4.1. Create Income Categories for the Participants¶

In [66]:
mean_income = df['income'].mean()
print(f"Mean: {mean_income}")

Mean: 36887.21835211268
In [68]:
df["Income_Category"] = np.where(df["income"] >= mean_income,
                                 "HIGH INCOME","LOW INCOME")
df.head()
Out[68]:
id sex degree income marital age height weight hrswrk Income_Category
0 1 MALE BACHELOR 60967.50 DIVORCED 53 72 190 60 HIGH INCOME
1 2 FEMALE BACHELOR 60967.50 MARRIED 26 60 97 40 HIGH INCOME
2 4 FEMALE BACHELOR 10161.25 MARRIED 56 68 160 20 LOW INCOME
3 14 FEMALE HIGH SCHOOL 17551.25 MARRIED 40 65 156 37 LOW INCOME
4 16 MALE HIGH SCHOOL 17551.25 MARRIED 56 66 210 6 LOW INCOME

4.2. Frequencies for Income Categories¶

In [70]:
freq = df["Income_Category"].value_counts()
freq
Out[70]:
Income_Category
LOW INCOME     637
HIGH INCOME    357
Name: count, dtype: int64

4.3. Descriptive Statistics¶

In [72]:
df.drop(columns = ['id']).describe()
Out[72]:
income age height weight hrswrk
count 994.000000 994.000000 994.000000 994.000000 994.000000
mean 36887.218352 44.488934 67.414487 181.318913 42.640845
std 34702.256068 13.126865 4.037741 41.641615 14.394974
min 369.500000 19.000000 57.000000 90.000000 1.000000
25% 15703.750000 33.000000 64.000000 150.000000 38.000000
50% 27712.500000 44.000000 67.000000 175.000000 40.000000
75% 49882.500000 55.000000 70.000000 205.000000 50.000000
max 158656.952000 79.000000 79.000000 410.000000 89.000000
In [74]:
bins = [19, 35, 45, 55, float('inf')]
labels = ["YOUNG", "YOUNG ADULT", "MIDDLE ADULT", "OLDER ADULTS"]
df['Age_Cat'] = pd.cut(df['age'],
                       bins = bins,
                       labels = labels,
                       right = False)
In [75]:
df.head()
Out[75]:
id sex degree income marital age height weight hrswrk Income_Category Age_Cat
0 1 MALE BACHELOR 60967.50 DIVORCED 53 72 190 60 HIGH INCOME MIDDLE ADULT
1 2 FEMALE BACHELOR 60967.50 MARRIED 26 60 97 40 HIGH INCOME YOUNG
2 4 FEMALE BACHELOR 10161.25 MARRIED 56 68 160 20 LOW INCOME OLDER ADULTS
3 14 FEMALE HIGH SCHOOL 17551.25 MARRIED 40 65 156 37 LOW INCOME YOUNG ADULT
4 16 MALE HIGH SCHOOL 17551.25 MARRIED 56 66 210 6 LOW INCOME OLDER ADULTS
In [77]:
freq = df['Age_Cat'].value_counts()
freq
Out[77]:
Age_Cat
YOUNG           273
OLDER ADULTS    257
YOUNG ADULT     234
MIDDLE ADULT    230
Name: count, dtype: int64

4.4. FREQUENCY DISTRIBUTION TABLE¶

In [79]:
df.describe(include = ["object","category"])
Out[79]:
sex degree marital Income_Category Age_Cat
count 994 994 994 994 994
unique 2 5 5 2 4
top MALE HIGH SCHOOL MARRIED LOW INCOME YOUNG
freq 507 491 480 637 273

freq = df['degree'].value_counts()
freq
Out[80]:
degree
HIGH SCHOOL       491
BACHELOR          209
GRADUATE          125
JUNIOR COLLEGE     92
LT HIGH SCHOOL     77
Name: count, dtype: int64
In [ ]:
